statement: For V3, within-family matched-sample inference (paired bootstrap CIs and paired permutation/sign-flip tests over sample-level deltas) will produce materially different and more reliable conclusions than comparing per-experiment aggregate means, because it respects the repeated-measures structure (shared samples and shared evidence pools) and reduces variance.
rationale: The V3 matrix shares a fixed set of sample ordinals and evidence across configurations, creating a natural paired design. Paired estimators and nonparametric resampling tests make fewer assumptions than parametric tests and are better behaved at n=30. Mixed-effects models can further partition sample/evidence variance and improve interpretability via partial pooling.
keyAssumptions: Sample ordinals are meaningfully matched across experiments (same underlying sample instances or equivalent sampling process).
Within-family contrasts are well-defined (clear baseline vs variant mapping).
Per-sample aggregates summarize the underlying evidence-level responses without discarding critical structure.
Permutation exchangeability holds approximately at the chosen unit of analysis (or can be made to hold via stratification).
validationCriteria: For each family, compute paired deltas for a primary endpoint panel (abstain_rate, singleton_rate, mean_subset_size, entropy/compression, conflict).
Show that paired bootstrap CI widths are meaningfully tighter than unpaired bootstrap CI widths for the same endpoints.
Run paired permutation tests for at least one primary endpoint per family and confirm qualitative agreement with bootstrap inference.
Fit a simple mixed-effects model with random intercepts for sample and evidence (or bundle) and confirm it yields consistent directionality with paired delta inference.
critiquePoints: If sample matching is weak or sample ordinals encode different draws per experiment, pairing could bias inference and understate uncertainty.
Some metrics are non-linear aggregates (e.g., DST conflict) and may require careful unit-of-analysis choices to avoid misleading CIs.
Small n=30 makes mixed-model random-slope specifications brittle; overly complex models can mislead more than help.
Multiplicity remains a major risk: many endpoints and families can generate false positives without explicit correction or prioritization.
questions: What is the canonical baseline for each family (especially families with >2 variants), and should baselines differ by model?
Do we treat evidence-level responses as independent within sample, or do we explicitly model within-sample correlation?
Which endpoints are primary vs secondary for V3 reporting, and what multiple-comparison policy should we enforce?
